speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing great. What was your name at birth, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- did you ever use a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: A nickname? No, I used Moishe, Moses, Misha.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, can you try to speak a little bit louder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used Moishe, Misha, Moses, and Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little bit louder.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I need a long sentence basically.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you sold your house, did you just pack up? Did you get rid of your furniture? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sold my furniture to my son. And I moved in with my son for three days. And then I moved here.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I was just in between moving. Everything was packed up. And the mover came, and got the stuff, and then I-- then I moved here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you drive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my niece's husband drove me down here. He volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: How nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How are we doing? This is February 23, 1996. We will be speaking to Mark M-A-R-K, Zilist Z-I-L-I-S-T survivor. I am the interviewer, Meta Joy Jacoby. We are in Lauderhill, Florida, USA. The language in this interview will be English. This is tape one. The date is February 23, 1996. I am Meta Joy Jacoby, M-E-T-A J-O-Y J-A-C-O-B-Y. And I will be interviewing Mark Zilist-- M-A-R-K Z-I-L-I-S-T. We are in Lauderhill, Florida, USA. And this interview will be conducted in English. This is tape one. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark Zilist.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe Zilist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a nickname when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Misha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Misha? And your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: October, 14, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you are now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a small town in Ukraine called Romanov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Romanov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Romanov, which is near Zhytomyr.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zhytomyr.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Ukraine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like in Romanov?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mainly a farmer work. And it was a small town. It was just a few stores. My father was running a grocery store that was belonged to the government. And he was the manager.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many Jews do you think were in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, roughly 5,000, 6,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of a population of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Romanov. Of--

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the total?

speaker: interviewer
text: So about 50% of it was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of the Jews-- you said they were farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Jews were farmers, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And businesspeople. It was under government supervision.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were relations between Jews and non-Jews in Romanov?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a good relationship, because, see, we were under the communist regime. And nobody was allowed to discriminate against anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to a public school, Russian school, that I finished sixth grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your relations with the non-Jews in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no problem, no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: The teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teachers-- the schoolmates-- there was no problem-- no anti-Semitism or no discrimination.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not for my age. My parents were probably--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any--

speaker: interviewee
text: --years back. I've heard some grownups talking about it from years back but not during the communist regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said that they had-- they were mistreated and looked down up on. But that was before the communist regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was comfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my house, we spoke Yiddish and Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- were your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family father and mother were religious, but not orthodox religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe what you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that-- is that-- see, under the communist regime, we were not allowed to practice religion. So the Jewish people would get together in each other's house, and have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prayers, and had minyans, and they had prayers and so forth. And Friday night, they'd get together for Shabbos. But it was all illegal and all undercover. But at the same token, nobody was bothered about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What rituals were practiced in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say that again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What rituals were practiced in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is rituals?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother light Friday night candles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father said kaddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was food? Was it a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kosher. It was a kosher home.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a kosher butcher in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A [NON-ENGLISH], yes. And my mother did all the cooking, all the baking. And it was-- it was kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was Shabbos observed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos was observed only in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning that Friday night we had Shabbos dinner. And my mother lit candles. But as far as the outside is concerned, if my father had to work on Shabbos, he did it, only because the government told him to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What literature was in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish paper and Russian paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother-- did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother was a housewife. And only my father worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had an older sister, step-sister. She was roughly about six or seven year-- seven years older than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was she a step-sister, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my father's second marriage. His first wife died. And he had the daughter by his first wife. And then he married my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], Rochelle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the outbreak of the war, you were 12 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: 11 Years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: 11 Years old. And your sister would have been.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1929-- almost 12 years. I was 12 and 1/2 years old. So my sister was 18, 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was in the last year of school.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was also a student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about Nazism before the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: None at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew nothing about what was happening to  Jews in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I did know when the war broke out in 1939, I heard about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard that the war broke out in Poland and that the Germans were killing Jews. And I'm sure that my parents were more knowledgeable of them than I was, because I was just a youngster. But I remember some of it vaguely. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A year or two before the war broke out, that's the first time that we had a radio in the house. And the radio consisted of the speaker only. And whatever the government broadcasted on the radio, that's what we heard. We couldn't tune in to no stations. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a well for water with hothouses outside for toilets. It was a very [NON-ENGLISH] old-fashioned town.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were being prepared for a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the rabbi, yeah, by a rabbi a religious person that would come to the house and teach me the Torah. But half of the time when he came, I-- I wasn't home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to learn. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like doing as a  child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked school. And I liked to play outside. And I had a rabbit as my pet. I raised rabbits. And when my mother would buy-- see, my mother used to go to the-- to the open-air market and by live chickens. And we put them in cages. And I used to like to feed the chickens. We-- we fed them to fatten them up and then take them, and slaughter them, and have them for dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you were being prepared for bar mitzvah, how many times a week or what was the routine that the rabbi came to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi would come to the house two or three times a week. Out of the two or three times, I was with him once or twice a week. I remember that vaguely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to anyone else's bar mitzvahs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not allowed to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the German invasion was what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941-- either June or July? Probably July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after they occupied the town, shortly after, one morning they walk-- they came into the house. They knocked on the door. They woke us up. They told us to get dressed and go outside. They round up all the Jews from town. And they put us in a large building like a-- where soldiers are-- like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: An armory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Armory, exactly. And after they round up all the Jews, they started to sort out professionals. By that-- what I mean by that is tailors, shoemakers, and young children up to a certain age. And they let us go. And the rest of them, they killed all in one day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when they let me go, I was left by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they-- where were these Jews killed, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] They were led to the woods. And there was a grave dug-- massive graves dug. And they were killed. And they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard the shots. And they never came back. And we were told that's what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- after I was let go, I stayed with one of my neighbors. He was a tailor. So they let him go and his whole family. And that's what they talked about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never had a chance to go to the grave. But this is what we were told. And we heard shots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family and all the rest of the Jewish people in my town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that day you lost your mother, your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was August 25, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with my neighbor until they decided to round up all the professionals and children and kill them, too. So when they came in to-- to the man that I stayed with, and they told them to go outside, I hid in back of a door. And they didn't see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after they left, I went-- I crawled on top of the attic in the house. And I stayed there for a day and a half. I was petrified. I was scared to go out. I was scared to move. I was scared to do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had to go outside. When I go out, I was hiding in hot houses and abandoned houses. During that time, the Ukrainian police that was working with the Germans and the Nazis, they were searching the town for-- for Jews that was left over. And two times, they caught me. And they put me in jail. And during the day, they would round up women and children and put everybody in one cell. 5 o'clock in the evening, they would let us out to the woods. And they had a grave dug. And they would kill everybody. And I managed to run away both times.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- easing off-- we were led in the woods. And there was guards on the side and in the back. But I was easing off to the side, and easing off to the side, and just took off and ran. And either they saw it and didn't see it. Or, they didn't see me run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this happened two times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. The last time that I ran away, I decided not to go back in town, because it's-- I would have been killed too. So I decided to walk-- I was scared of the front line. So I decided to walk to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sat down, and I made up of facticious ID, ID with a Ukrainian or Russian name. And I put it in my pocket. And I just walked so many miles every day until it got dark. And then I would come to a village and beg for bread, beg for food, beg for a place to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if I didn't have a place to stay, I would sleep outside. It led on. It was like in the fall. So during the winter, I remember sleeping in the snow one night. And I never caught a cold. And this is how I kept on going unbeknown to me where I was going to wind up or what I was going to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, when you made these false papers for yourself, how did you know to do that? Did you have any guidance?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever challenge you? Did you have to show those papers to anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody ever asked me for ID. I passed by German soldiers on the road-- Ukrainian police, people, nobody ever stopped me for anything. I was dirty with torn up clothes. And I just looked like a homeless person. And they never paid attention to me. Then I was a kid, too. So they didn't-- they didn't think that I was Jewish. That's the main reason why I was never stopped

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you asked if you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only time that I was asked if I was Jewish is when I-- I reached the destination. I went into this man's house. And he had an office. He was in the lumber business.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on this trip, we never asked if you are Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the trip, no. Only one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get to that. About how many-- how many villages do you think you stayed in during all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 10, or maybe 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never knew any name of the places that I was in. Because I never was interested in the names. I was interested in two things, to survive, find a place to work, and survive the-- the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: One night, one-- one night, this man took me in to his house. And he knew that I was Jewish without even me telling him. And he kept me for two days. And the night before I-- I left, he told me to leave early in the morning. He sat down. And he talked to me for about two or three hours. He was a very religious man, a young man, but a very religious and very much read up in the Bible. And he had quotations from the Bible hanging on the walls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that how you knew he was religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: That and by talking to him. He quoted quotations from the Bible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What Bible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gentile Bible.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Christian Bible.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what religion he was. But any rate, he told me a lot what's going to happen to me in the future. And believe it or not, most of-- 90% of it came true.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me that I'm going to survive. [DRINKS] He told me that I was going to be successful. Above all, he told me not to take revenge. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he emphasized that very strongly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after all these years, I remember that. [DRINKS] And he told me that after I leave him, not to worry, that soon I will find a place to stay. And I did. And I got to this place-- to this man that had a-- he was in the lumber business. And I asked him for a job. And he gave me a job as a house boy. And I stayed with him maybe a few months. During the time that I stayed with him, he questioned me periodically if I was Jewish. And I told him no, no, and no. Until one day, I took the horses into the field-- into the pasture. And it was near the woods. And there was some other kids with horses, and cows, and the pasture. And some of the kids were playing with fire. And they set fire in the woods. So I took the horses. And I came home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I told him what happened. And he didn't say anything. A week later, I was in the barn. And he came in. And he says, there's German soldiers in my office. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They want to talk to you. Are you Jewish or not? I said, yes, I am. He says, do you want to face them? Or do you want to run away? I said, I'm going to face them. What made  you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The older I get, the more I believe that there was some spirit guiding me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What made me say that and what made me do that, I don't know. But this is what I did. So he took me into the office. I sat down by the table. And the Germans were across the table right in front of me. And the first thing they asked me if I smoke. And I told them no . And the next thing they asked me whether I know anything-- I know who set the fire. What it turned out to be is they came to investigate who set up the fire in the woods. And I told them I don't know who did it. But there was some more kids in there. And one of them set the fire. And I don't know who. But I don't smoke. And they turned me. They told me I can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your true identity was never an issue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] [DRINKS] It felt good in a way and bad in another way that I told the man-- I gave myself away. I told my man-- the man who I was. Luckily, he took me in the house. And  he sat down he and told-- after everybody left. And he told me. He says, look, he says, I had suspicion all along that you were Jewish. Now, that I know that you're Jewish, he said, I cannot keep you here. And the penalties was that if the Germans found a Jewish person in the house, they killed the whole family and burned the house down. He knew that. And he told me that. And I knew that. He says, but don't worry about it. He says, I'm not going to turn you in. I'm going to find you a farmer that is looking for a house boy. And I'll get you a job with him. You'll live with him. And I'll never tell nobody nothing about you. And that's what happened. He found me a place where the farmer needed a house boy. And I lived with him and worked with him-- got beating-- took beatings every day. He was a young farmer. He was a drinker. He was a womanizer. And whether he knew who I was or he didn't he didn't know I was, he took advantage of me. He taught me how to plow in the field. Then he would come out. And he would look and say, that's not straight. And he took the whip that I was whipping the horse. And-- [DRINKS] and he would whip me across the hand. I couldn't complain. I couldn't say anything, because I wanted to survive. Later on, as time passed, I was on this farm for at least two years or maybe more. But later, on as time passed by, he was found dead on another farm in a-- in a barn. He got drunk, or he got in a fight. And somebody killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his wife was left with two children. And I was the man of the house, and taking care of the farm, and everything that needs to be taken care of until the Germans started retreating. And one day, we saw the Germans retreating. And I was outside. And I had the horse and the wagon in back of the house. So when I saw that, I jumped in a wagon. And I took off running with the horse and wagon into the field. His wife and the two children were scared. And the Germans were shooting at me. And they never hit the horse, the wagon, or me.

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife got scared. And they had a hole dug in the middle of the room where they used to keep food in the summertime, because it is cold underneath the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was an earthen floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was an Earth floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: An Earth floor, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was for refrigeration or a cooler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So she took the children and should crawled into that hole trying to hide from the Germans. After I ran away, the Germans set the house on fire, And she got killed with the children-- suffocated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Was she good to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The wife was. Were you paid anything for this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your pay was food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just worked for room and board.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody slept in one room. I slept on the side near-- there was a brick open where she used to go to cooking. And there was a small-- small space between the wall and the oven. And that's where I slept on a board-- on a wooden board.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say so, yeah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Ukrainian name that you used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Valodka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Valodka. Did you have to change anything else about yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [DRINKS] The only other thing that I had to do was keep my hair close cut real close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I have curly hair. And gentile people in Europe don't have curly hair per se as a whole-- Ukrainians, Russians. So my curly hair would give me away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I cut my hair almost every week with the clippers. I had one of the farmers do that for me before it grew up-- it grew out and extended where it started curling. So it wasn't noticeable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I got all that knowledge as a kid to do all these things, I had the slightest-- I don't have the slightest idea. I don't know. Like I say, something was guiding me and telling me what to do and how to do it. And that's what I did until 1944 when the-- that night-- that day when the Germans was-- were retreating, I went in-- in a-- in a field. And the lady that I stayed with, she had a sister that lived-- she had a shack in the field. She lived with her husband and two children. So I came to her after I ran away. And  she told me that since everybody is dead and the houses burned down, I could stay with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed with her for a few days. And then word came that the Russians are in the village, which means that the town near the village was liberated. I got up one morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was scared to tell them where I was going. And I was scared to tell him I was Jewish. So I just got up one morning, and I left and didn't say nothing to anybody. I left, I went on the main road.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this, Mark?

speaker: interviewer
text: Winter, summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Summer. I got on the main road, and I was walking towards Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-S-T-R-O-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is in--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was Poland. And at times-- at time-- at times, it was Russian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But at the time during the war, it belonged to Poland. When I came to-- so when I was walking on the road, a wagon came. A farmer had a load of hay stacked up real high. And he was going towards-- towards that town-- towards Ostrog. I jumped up. I caught the back of the wagon. And I pulled myself up on top of the hay. And the man never knew that I was up there, because he was in the front of the hay controlling the horse and didn't see or didn't hear anything about it. As soon as we got on the outskirts of town, I jumped off the wagon. And I started walking into town. And he never knew I was there.
